# Transcrição: 04-Best Practices for Your Virtual Event

- **Fonte:** 04-Best Practices for Your Virtual Event
- **Duração:** 10:10
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

I cannot wait to dive into this training today as we're going to be talking through some of the best practices when it comes to hosting your mastermind virtually. Now, I did a few other trainings within this program on planning, creating and executing an in-person event and I would encourage you to go back and watch those if you haven't already, as they have some really helpful tips on preparing your mastermind agenda, creating assets that can support your event and how to communicate with your attendees, plus some other helpful strategies that can easily be translated into virtual elements. Now, if this is your first time meeting me, my name is Aubrey and I'm the Events Director here at Mastermind. I've been with Mastermind for about five years now and during that time I experienced many shifts in the industry, including when events shifted to being entirely virtual. During that transition, I gained valuable experience and insights in bringing virtual events to life and keeping the same energy and excitement that comes from in-person events, which is what I want to share with you in this training. Now, before you decide to do your mastermind virtually, it's really important to understand the different advantages and disadvantages that come with this decision. But let's start with the advantages first. Accessibility is one of the biggest advantages when it comes to hosting a mastermind virtually, as it allows participants from all over the world to join. You'll be able to expand your network and connect with people from all different types of backgrounds and nobody has to worry about traveling. Virtual masterminds are also much more cost effective than in-person, as you don't have to bother with finding a venue, catering, booking travel, or any of those other costs that come with planning live events. Familiarity used to be a disadvantage when it came to virtual events, as most people hadn't used Zoom or other online meeting platforms before. But it has become an advantage in today's industry, as Zoom is a very common platform for people to use in their day to day. Plus, it's free, so that makes it even better. Recording capabilities are also another great advantage if you're wanting to provide a recording of your mastermind to your attendees, as a Zoom platform easily allows for sessions to be recorded for anyone who isn't able to join. However, with every advantage, there are disadvantages, but when you're aware of them, you can actually maximize the advantages and take proactive action to minimize the disadvantages. This will allow you to truly create a valuable and engaging experience for your virtual attendees. So let's walk through some of the challenges you may face and how you can overcome them. The first is technical challenges. Even though most people are familiar with Zoom, you may have some who are not. So this is something to expect and prepare for. In order to prepare for it, first you will want to make sure that you yourself are very familiar with Zoom and its features or whichever online meeting platform you're using. You'll want to test your internet connection and make sure you aren't lagging before the event begins. Nothing is worse than when you're sharing something really important, only start freezing, and that can really take away from your guest experience, and we don't want that. We typically recommend being close to your internet connection or actually hard-wiring

## [03:02 - 06:06]

the computer you're using into your internet in order to avoid that. And another thing to test while you're at it is the quality of your microphone and your camera. You want to make sure your guests will be able to see and hear you clearly. Then you will want to make sure your attendees are familiar with Zoom before they join the meeting. That way you don't have to deal with any hiccups of the day and can just focus on providing an incredible event. One way that we do this is called Tech Check. This is where attendees join a Zoom prior to the event and test their own camera, video, and Zoom capabilities that will be used during your event, such as raising your hand or using the chat. This is also a great opportunity to connect face-to-face with your attendees before the event and allow them to ask any questions they may have. It also just helps put a face to the emails that they're receiving and makes them a little bit more excited about joining the virtual event. Now, it might not be necessary for your audience if you know that they're pros at using Zoom, but it's always an option just in case they aren't. Another challenge you might face is distractions. Since your attendees are not physically in the room with you, they will be subject to distractions in their own home and their office that you can't control. So when it comes to minimizing distractions, it's not so much what you do, but rather helping your attendees control their own environment before the event starts. I would suggest creating a pre-event checklist for your attendees. This is just a list of suggestions that you can provide your attendees to help them get the most out of the event. We like to include things such as playful out. This encourages students to fully commit and give their all to an event. We like to say, the more you put in, the more you'll get out. The next item we like to give our attendees is set an intention. By having your attendees pick a goal for what they want to get out of the event, they'll be more engaged with the content during the event. Another great item to add to your checklist is plan ahead. We understand that people have busy lives outside of virtual events, but by asking them to plan ahead for things such as making sure dinner is handled, laundry's done, the kids are at grandma's, or simply just letting the people in their life know what they're doing, they'll be better equipped to set distractions aside, find a quiet place, and truly focus and engage during the day of the event. And lastly, we often ask our guests to make the time. Now, it sounds simple, but time is one of the biggest excuses that people have today. There never seems to be enough of it, but we make the time for the things that are important to us. So, remind them why this is important to them. This will really encourage them to make changes to their schedule and ensure that they're actively engaged at the virtual event. Now, based on your event, you may want to add more things to your checklist. And I would encourage you to. Just remember to keep things simple as you don't want to overwhelm your attendees before they even arrive to the event. If you're doing a tech check, this is a great time to present this checklist. However, if you aren't doing that, you may choose to just include it in one of your reminder emails or if you call your attendees to confirm their attendance, you can also share it there. Just choose to share the information in whichever form best aligns with you and your event.

## [06:06 - 09:09]

Now, similar to managing distractions, one of the other disadvantages of virtual events is managing your attendees engagement and energy levels. You'll want to prepare for people to have lower engagement during a virtual event compared to a live event. Sitting in front of a computer for a long period of time can often lead to screen fatigue, so it's crucial to encourage active participation. How you do so will be very dependent on the nature of your event and your brand. For us, we like to do what we call energy cranks, where between transitions, we'll blast some music, get everybody to stand up and just stand and move around, as this encourages attendees to just stand up and move their body so that they can wake up. Another example is how Tony Robbins has attendees create power postures. But if neither of those make sense for your event, you could always just do a simple stretch break where you quickly have everyone stand up and do some easy stretches. You should also plan for moments in your agenda that encourages attendees active contribution and participation. You don't want to talk at them the whole time. The most powerful part of Masterminds are the connections and discussions that you have, and that doesn't have to be a lost component in a virtual setting. Breakout rooms are a great feature that Zoom has. By utilizing these, you can create moments for smaller group discussions or activities. But to make sure that they're impactful, you'll want to make sure you set the expectation of what should be discussed in that room and how much time they'll have before putting them into the rooms. Zoom also has many other features similar to breakout rooms that can really elevate your event. We created a short tutorial on these features in this program, so make sure to check that out and see what you can incorporate into your own virtual event. You should also plan for some Q&A opportunities throughout your teaching sessions. You can either accomplish this by asking your attendees to drop the questions in the chat, or simply have them unmute and ask their question. I do have a tip on this, though. In your Zoom security settings, you can turn off the ability for your participants to freely unmute themselves. This allows you to better control the conversations that are happening during your event, if that makes sense for you and your brand. On the flip side of Q&A opportunities, you may also want to encourage aha moments. This is where your attendees share any breakthroughs that they had from the day. This is a great way to build rapport and excitement in the room, but it also can be an opportunity to get testimonials that can be used for future events. Another great way to encourage engagement is incorporating visual aids, such as slides, videos, or other items. This can be really helpful in highlighting important topics and just keeping your attendees interested and focused. You may even choose to send out physical items, such as workbooks, branded pens, notebooks, or other things that just could get them excited about attending the event virtually. By doing this, you will also increase your show up rates, as it's a great physical reminder for the event. Now, this next tip may seem like it's common sense, but it truly can make or break the engagement at a virtual event. And it's your presentation skills. You will be leading the room, so it's very important that you set the expectation and lead by example. If you want your attendees to have high energy, you have to have high energy.

## [09:09 - 10:10]

There's actually a couple lessons inside of the Mastermind Business System that focus on effective speaking skills and embracing your speaking style. We're going to link them below this video so that they're easy to find. And I would encourage you to go through and elevate your presentation skills to really bring a new layer of life to your event. Now, if there was only one strategy that I could give you for keeping virtual events lively and engaging, it would be to make your attendees feel seen. Say their names, acknowledge their videos, be active in the chat. I'm sure that you've seen Dean shout out people on Zoom before or compliment their cat that keeps walking across the screen. But there's a purpose behind that. And it's to make your virtual attendees feel like they are right there in the room with you. Now, I hope that this training has showed you that even though virtual events may not replace being in person, they can be a fantastic way to run a Mastermind if executed correctly. By implementing these practices on top of the strategies I provided in the other trainings, you can truly create a dynamic and interactive virtual Mastermind.
